key_insight: Physics-informed stacked residual neural networks can accurately model hot deformation and DRX in a Mo-rich titanium alloy by embedding constitutive laws directly into learning.
engineering_relevance: The approach enables more reliable process design and thermomechanical simulation for forging and hot-working of advanced titanium alloys across wide temperature and strain-rate ranges.
method: Train shared residual encoders on experimental flow-stress curves from 800–1050 °C and 0.01–10 s⁻¹.
Enforce physics constraints for thermal softening, strain-rate sensitivity, strain hardening, and post-peak softening via automatic differentiation.
Use a DRX-aware dual-output model with JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and latent-feature fusion to link recrystallization to stress.
evidence_signal: The DRX-Aware STAR-PINN achieved RMSE 11.69 MPa, MAE 4.83 MPa, R² 0.9850, and cross-validated RMSE 12.47 ± 0.26 MPa while reproducing flow curves, DRX kinetics, and Zener-Hollomon trends.
watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and coverage of experimental calibration data, so extrapolation beyond the tested temperature, strain-rate, and alloy space should be treated cautiously.
paper_title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy
